data.title: Recruit Holdings: To Buy Back Up to Y45B of Own Shares on Tuesday
Carma Is Aiming to Become a Household Brand, Chair Says
Carma Has Appointed REA Group's CEO as Its Board Chair
Australia's Carma Eyeing Funding Options Including Potential IPO — Interview
data.description:
(07:22 GMT) *Australia's Carma Eyeing Funding Options Including Potential IPO
(07:22 GMT) *Used-Car Dealer Carma Aiming for Larger Slice of US$65 Billion Market
(07:22 GMT) *Carma Attracting Investors Enthusiastic for Model, CEO Says
(07:23 GMT) *Carma Has Appointed REA Group's CEO as Its Board Chair
(07:23 GMT) *Carma Is Aiming to Become a Household Brand, Chair Says
(07:23 GMT) Australia's Carma Eyeing Funding Options Including Potential IPO — Interview

By Stuart Condie

SYDNEY--Australian used-car refurbisher and dealer Carma is eyeing a range of funding options as it targets a larger slice of the country's US$65 billion market, including a potential stock-market listing.

Sydney-based Carma, which is backed by venture-capital and private-equity firms including New York-based Tiger Global and Australia's Regal Funds Management, has raised more than US$90 million in funding since 2021. That includes a convertible-note offering earlier this year worth 30 million Australian dollars, or about US$19.6 million.

The company's business model differs from typical used-car yards. Carma buys second-hand vehicles from consumers before refurbishing them in a suburban industrial unit, using a process reminiscent of how they were originally assembled on a factory line. The cars are then put up for sale online.

Carma is now assessing how best to raise capital as it looks to expand beyond its Sydney base.

"There are lots of investors that are enthusiastic about this model, so there's lots of opportunity for funding," said Chief Executive Lachlan MacGregor. "We've funded through private markets to date and there's obviously options for us to continue funding the business."

One option could be through an initial public offering, according to board chair Owen Wilson. Wilson, the chief executive of News Corp-controlled property classifieds provider REA Group, said that an IPO by the end of 2025 was possible.

"In five or so years, we want this to be a household brand in Australia," said Wilson, who will step down at REA later this year and is also on the board of Dubai-based property portal Property Finder.

Carma's pitch is that its vetting process gives customers additional peace of mind when buying a used car. Its online platform, adorned with galleries of images shot at its in-house studio, is aimed at time-poor, digital-savvy consumers.

Vehicle sellers are quoted a price generated by Carma's proprietary AI-driven model, while buyers get a seven-day trial period after purchase.

This year's convertible notes offering was led by Regal and Tiger. Existing investors General Catalyst and Five V Capital also participated.

Carma, which wouldn't give a valuation or any growth metrics, used the proceeds to expand its vehicle inventory and to support the expansion of its production facilities. It has increased its headcount to more than 80, from about 50 in March.

MacGregor said he was inspired to start the business by the success of U.S. platform Carvana. Founded in 2012, Carvana listed in 2017 and generated US$4.84 billion in revenue in its last fiscal quarter, up 42% compared with the same period a year ea.

That was on about a 1.5% share of the U.S. used-car market, Carvana CEO Ernie Garcia III said in last month's call with investors to discuss the company's second-quarter performance.

Carma estimates Australia's used-car market to be worth about A$100 billion. The company generated A$68.2 million in revenue in 2024 from 2,000 vehicle sales, suggesting plenty of runway for growth if it can match Carvana's market share.

"Their ambitions are not going to stick at 1.5% and neither are ours. There's this great opportunity for us to be a large player," MacGregor said.

Write to Stuart Condie at stuart.condie@wsj.com

data.title: GSK to Receive $370 Million in U.S. Patent Litigation Settlement
data.description:
By Anthony O. Goriainoff

GSK said it would receive $370 million as part of a U.S. patent settlement between CureVac and BioNTech related to messenger mRNA-based Covid-19 vaccines.

The U.K. pharmaceutical giant said Friday that, under the terms of an existing license agreement with CureVac, it would receive an upfront payment of $320 million in cash, with the rest of the money through an amendment to the deal with CureVac. It will also receive a 1% royalty on future U.S. sales of influenza, Covid-19 and other related combination mRNA vaccine products made by BioNTech and Pfizer.

The payment will be recorded as other operating income in GSK's third-quarter financial results, it said.

Biotech company CureVac said Thursday that it had reached a series of agreements with BioNTech and Pfizer to resolve all pending patent litigation in the U.S. with GSK related to mRNA-based Covid-19 vaccines. Under the agreements both it and GSK would receive a payment of $740 million, CureVac said.

GSK said the settlement didn't affect the enforcement of its own patents against Pfizer and BioNTech in the U.S. or Europe.

"GSK will continue with its litigation against BioNTech and Pfizer for infringement of GSK's patents", it said.

Pfizer didn't immediately respond to a request for comment. BioNTech, which denies the allegations raised by CureVac or GSK, said the settlement didn't represent an admission of liability.

London-listed GSK shares in early trade were up 22.50 pence, or 1.6%, at 1,405.50 pence.

Write to Anthony O. Goriainoff at anthony.orunagoriainoff@dowjones.com

data.title: European Luxury Sector Could Start to Pick Up This Year — Market Talk
data.description: European luxury companies could start to see a rebound after weak results, Deutsche Bank analysts write in a research note. The second-quarter earnings season didn't throw up many surprises, with most luxury brands seeing a sequential slowdown from the previous three months, the analysts say. Companies that are more exposed to tourism and to lower-income consumers faced greater challenges, they add. Despite current difficulties, Deutsche Bank expects a sequential sales improvement in the second half. "The investor view on the sector remains largely negative but there are early signs of this starting to change," DB says. (andrea.figueras@wsj.com)

data.title: Airbus Has Work to Do to Meet Full-Year Deliveries Target — Market Talk
data.description: Airbus delivered 373 commercial aircraft in the first seven months of the year, down 7% on year, a figure that is still far from its full-year target of 820 aircraft, RBC Europe analysts say. To meet this target, the European planemaker will need to deliver an average of 89 aircraft a month until December, they say. The group ended the second quarter with 60 A320 gliders--aircraft without engines. Assuming it has delivered those by year-end, then Airbus would need to deliver 77 aircraft a month to meet the full-year target of 820, the analysts add. This monthly amount is achievable for Airbus, they say. Shares are down 0.1% at 177.18 euros. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

data.title: NN Group's Solvency 2 Performance Could Drive Upgraded Buyback — Market Talk
data.description: NN Group's first-half solvency 2 ratio of 208% will excite investors, KBW analyst Michele Ballatore writes. The Dutch insurance and asset-management group's solvency 2 result solidly beat expectations, he adds. In the absence of any material shocks in the second half of the year, an upgraded share buyback could be closer than expected, he adds. Elsewhere, NN Group's operating capital generation beat consensus views by 7%, driven by a better-than-expected performance in its NL Life division, he adds. Shares trade up 1% at 61.46 euros. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

data.title: Euro zone bond yields set for second weekly decline
data.description:
Euro zone bond yields headed for a second weekly decline on Friday, sticking to a seasonally tight trading range, as investors mulled the diverging interest rate outlooks for the European Central Bank and U.S. Federal Reserve.

Traders expect the ECB to leave euro zone rates at 2% until well into 2026, while shifts in U.S. data have prompted a surge in bets on at least two Fed rate cuts by the end of this year.

"As both data and supply calendars are completely depleted today, we expect a modest intra-day range today with yields tentatively drifting higher," Commerzbank analysts said.

German 10-year yields (DE10YT=RR) rose 1 basis point to 2.643%, but were set for a 3.4-bp fall this week, the second weekly decline in a row. Yields have stuck largely to a 2.6-2.7% range in the past few trading days.

Meanwhile, U.S. President Donald Trump, in his quest to reshape Fed leadership to conform more closely with his own views that rates need to fall sharply, on Thursday nominated Council of Economic Advisers Chairman Stephen Miran to fill a newly vacant seat on the central bank board.

Two-year U.S. Treasury yields (US2YT=RR) have fallen by 21 bps since the start of August alone, compared with a 1-bp dip in two-year Schatz yields, which on Friday were 1 bp higher on the day at 1.93%. The spread between the two (DE2US2=RR) is around 181 bps, just above last week's four-month low around 178 bps.

The Bank of England on Thursday cut UK rates by a quarter point, as expected, but policymakers are so divided on the outlook for inflation and growth that the decision was subject to a second vote for the first time in the central bank's history.

data.title: U.S. Battery Startup Lyten Buys Northvolt's Remaining European Assets
data.description:
By Dominic Chopping

U.S. battery startup Lyten has agreed to buy the remaining European assets of bankrupt Swedish battery maker Northvolt.

Northvolt filed for bankruptcy in March after it failed to secure the financial backing needed to continue operating, but the bankruptcy trustee revealed in June that three possible buyers had been found, including a foreign investor that had bid to take over the entire operation.

Northvolt has continued to operate since its bankruptcy, but on a scaled-down basis, after the bankruptcy trustee reached an agreement in principle with key stakeholders earlier this year. Parts of the business have already been sold off, including its heavy industry business to Scania and a Swedish battery plant to joint venture partner Volvo Car.

Lyten, a Silicon Valley startup developing lithium-sulfur batteries which are seen as more environmentally friendly than lithium-ion batteries, is backed by auto giant Stellantis, logistics provider FedEx and industrial conglomerate Honeywell, among others.

It said late Thursday it has agreed to buy Northvolt's main factory and research and development facility which are both located in Sweden, as well as the site of a future so-called gigafactory in Germany and all intellectual property.

The price Lyten has paid wasn't disclosed, but it confirmed the acquisition includes assets valued at around $5 billion.

"Lyten's mission is to be the leading supplier of clean, locally sourced and manufactured batteries and energy storage systems in both North America and Europe," Lyten Chief Executive and Co-Founder Dan Cook said in a statement.

"The acquisition of Northvolt's assets brings the facilities and Swedish talent to accelerate this mission by years."

The company plans to rehire a significant portion of the previously laid-off workforce and will assess staffing needs site by site as it restarts and scales operations.

Northvolt had a workforce of around 5,500 when it filed for bankruptcy and the previous deal to continue operating on a scaled-down basis meant around 1,700 workers stayed on in Sweden.

Lyten expects to immediately restart operations at the Swedish sites upon closing and is working with the German government to continue developing the site there. Talks with Canadian officials are also continuing, as it pursues the acquisition of Northvolt's North American gigafactory under construction in Quebec.

The startup had previously announced the acquisition of Northvolt's battery manufacturing facility in California, its battery energy storage system manufacturing facility in Poland and its battery energy storage system product and intellectual property portfolio.

Founded in 2015, Lyten has received more than $625 million in equity investment and secured letters of intent for $650 million in financing from the Export Import Bank of the U.S.

Write to Dominic Chopping at dominic.chopping@wsj.com

data.title: HA Sustainable Infrastructure Capital Inc reports results for the quarter ended June 30 - Earnings Summary
data.description:
• HA Sustainable Infrastructure Capital Inc reported quarterly adjusted earnings of 60 cents​​ per share for the quarter ended June 30, lower than the same quarter last year, when the company reported EPS of 63 cents. The mean expectation of fifteen analysts for the quarter was for earnings of 63 cents per share. Wall Street expected results to range from 59 cents to 68 cents per share.

  • Revenue fell 9.3% to $85.69 million from a year ago; analysts expected $92.12 million.

  • HA Sustainable Infrastructure Capital Inc's reported EPS for the quarter was 74 cents​.

  • The company reported quarterly net income of $98.45 million.

  • HA Sustainable Infrastructure Capital Inc shares had fallen by 9.2% this quarter and lost 9.1% so far this year.

FORECAST CHANGES

  • The mean earnings estimate of analysts had fallen by about 0.2% in the last three months.​

  • In the last 30 days, four analysts negatively revised earnings estimates

RECOMMENDATIONS

  • The current average analyst rating on the shares is "buy" and the breakdown of recommendations is 14 "strong buy" or "buy," 2 "hold" and no "sell" or "strong sell." The average consensus recommendation for the investment management & fund operators peer group is also "buy"

  • Wall Street's median 12-month price target for HA Sustainable Infrastructure Capital Inc is $39.00, about 37.5% above its last closing price of $24.38

This summary was machine generated from LSEG data August 8 at 07:13 a.m. UTC. ​All figures in US dollars unless otherwise stated.
